How do you stand out from the dozens of other applications?"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"If you have a resume that reads very generic, you will not grab much attention. A resume where the hiring manager would have trouble recalling anything specific about your experience, even after carefully reading everything you wrote. A resume where each of your work experiences has an almost identical description of what you did: you built things with technologies and shipped projects that were supposedly important."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"In this chapter, we\u2019ll cover how you can have your resume stand out by being specific about the work you did. How using numbers can make your work experience shine and grab the attention of hiring managers. We\u2019ll also go through other ways to stand out, like tailoring your resume to the job posting, and how different companies look for different things."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Let\u2019s get started by how to talk better about the impact of your work."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.h2,{id:"results-impact-and-your-contribution",children:"Results, Impact and Your Contribution"}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:["When listing your work and project experiences, focus on what you achieved, as opposed to what you did. For the achievements, try to quantify these with the impact and (business) results. A framework you could use is \u201CAccomplished {impact} as measured by {number} by doing {specific contribution}\u201D. This is similar to ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/20140929001534-24454816-my-personal-formula-for-a-better-resume/",children:"the structure Google encourages"})," for resumes. You don't need to use the exact same wording. However, do make the impact clear, what your contribution was, and add specifics where you can."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"You want to convey that you are self-sufficient, that your work made a difference on your team, and that you are aware of your work's impact. To do so, edit your accomplishments with these points in mind:"}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.ul,{children:[`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Use numbers"}),". Quantify your impact wherever you can. Most resumes do not contain numbers: if you add these specifics, you will stand out. Instead of saying ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"\u201CBuilt a tool widely adopted by the company\u201D"}),", say ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"\u201CLed a team of 3 developers to build a dependency injection framework that was adopted by 15 teams and all 50+ developers at the company\u201D"}),". Numbers can be several things: number of people on the team, lines of code, code coverage % before and after, SLA changes, revenue generated by the project. They can be number of users, number of installs, number of five-star ratings, number of customer support tickets you proactively resolved, and many others."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Use active language"})," that shows what you have ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"done"})," and how you have been proactive. Use active verbs like \u201Cled\u201D, \u201Cmanaged\u201D, \u201Cdrove\u201D, \u201Cimproved\u201D, \u201Crolled out\u201D over passive ones like \u201Cimproving\u201D or \u201Crolling out\u201D."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Mention specific languages and technologies"})," that you used towards the end of your description. Impact and your contribution are more important to convey than the technologies. However, it\u2019s worth calling out what tools you\u2019ve used. Mentioning technologies in this context is more powerful for hiring managers and interviewers who are reading your resume in detail. Make sure that these technologies overlap with the ones you listed in your standalone Languages & Technologies section in your resume."]}),`
`]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"You stand out from the crowd by talking about the impact of your work and how you contributed to it, not just what you did or were responsible for"}),". The more senior you are, the more of an expectation this is, but doing so will make you stand out in all cases. Your resume should showcase how you have consciously and proactively added value through your actions. People who do this are the ones sought after\u2014developers who follow directions are a dime a dozen."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)("table",{className:"inside-out",children:(0,e.jsx)("tbody",{children:(0,e.jsx)("tr",{children:(0,e.jsxs)("td",{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"From the inside out: how do I come up with bullet points for my resume?"})}),(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:["Andy Lester, is a manager, lead, software engineer and the creator of ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"https://beyondgrep.com/",children:"ack"}),". He wrote the book ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"http://pragmaticurl.com/land-the-tech-job-you-love-ttr",children:"Land the Tech Job You Love"})," after many years of and shifting through countless resumes and interviewing candidates. In this book, this is what he advises on writing bullet points that grab the attention of the hiring manager reading your resume:"]}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Coming up with your bullets is the toughest part of the entire r\xE9sum\xE9 process. I recommend that you start it early; just scribble ideas on a sheet of paper that you carry with you. Don\u2019t worry about order or phrasing at this early phase. Write down everything you can remember doing, and worry about pruning later. It\u2019s far easier to get rid of extra information than to come up with one more bullet at the last minute. Instead, focus on the story, not specific buzzwords."})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsxs)(o.em,{children:["R\xE9sum\xE9 books often talk about the importance of using \u201Caction words\u201D in your bullet points. They\u2019ll list pages of words you can use in your r\xE9sum\xE9 to sound like you were effective: analyzed, compiled, coordinated, drafted, devised, implemented, blah blah blah. Although it\u2019s true that you want to use active verbs, as I noted earlier, ",(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"don\u2019t get hung up on which snazzy word you use. Instead, focus on the details of the work, getting as specific as possible."})]})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Details include numbers. If you can quantify some value that comes from the work you did, it gives weight to the value that provided. Even if you weren\u2019t the only person working on a project, you can still discuss your involvement. Here are some examples:"})}),(0,e.jsxs)(o.ul,{children:[`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Increased traffic to website 50% over six months by (and list the actions you took to make it happen)"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Led teams of four to six programmers"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Created new task tracking system that reduced schedule creep by 40%"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Installed new routers that increased throughput 25%, virtually eliminating the 20% of help desk calls related to network responsiveness"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Reduced open ticket backlog from 500 to 20 in three months."})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Refactored codebase to take advantage of standard C++ libraries, reducing total LOC from 100,000 to 70,000"})}),`
`]})]})})})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Talking about impact and your accomplishments is one of the most underrated approaches in developer resumes. Several senior developers have come to the same conclusion:"}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.ul,{children:[`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"\u201CEffective resumes need to contain two things: responsibilities and accomplishments. The first tells the reader what your job was; the second, what your results were. Unfortunately, most people fail at the accomplishments part.\u201D"})," From the article ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"https://jacobian.org/2020/may/8/engineering-resume-accomplishments/",children:"What accomplishments sound like on software engineering resumes"})," by developer and co-creator of Django Jacob-Kaplan Moss."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:["Are you an Implementer, a Solver, or a Finder? Senior engineers are not implementers, and your resume should convey that you are a Solver or a Finder, argues software developer Itamar Turner-Trauring in the article ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"https://codewithoutrules.com/2020/05/18/job-search-skills/",children:"To get a better programming job, explain your problem-solving skills"}),"."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"\u201CYour goal should be to have at least one number in each bullet point, supporting the story that the text tells. So few resumes have any sort of numbers or statistics on them, you'll put your resume ahead of 90% of the other applicants' resumes.\u201D"})," in ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"https://blog.petdance.com/2011/11/14/track-your-stats-like-a-pro-athlete-to-give-your-resume-power/",children:"Track your professional stats like a pro athlete to give your resume power"}),", an article by manager, lead and software engineer Andy Lester and author of ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"http://pragmaticurl.com/land-the-tech-job-you-love-ttr",children:"Land the Tech Job You Love"}),"."]}),`
`]}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Before and after: results and impact"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Before:"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:w,alt:"Image represents a text-based description of a software engineer's experience at a company called Gibbery.  The top line states the job title, 'Software Engineer at Gibbery'. Below this, a bulleted list details four key accomplishments. The first bullet point describes working on the Billing team and developing microservices. The second bullet point highlights the automation of error handling in RESTful API services, resulting in a reduction of client support tickets. The third bullet point focuses on improving the customer experience of the MyTrinn platform by integrating third-party APIs like Zoom Rooms and Square.  Finally, the fourth bullet point details reverse engineering and analysis of undocumented legacy code to understand its business logic, clean and refactor it, ultimately creating a new billing-focused service.  There are no diagrams, URLs, or parameters present; the information is purely textual and presented as a concise summary of professional experience.",width:"695",height:"205"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"While this description is not that bad, it does not talk about specifics that someone who has not worked at the company could understand. It also had no numbers. While a colleague might know what the MyTrinn platform is, and how challenging the work could have been, the recruiter/hiring manager would have no idea. The last sentence isn\u2019t that bad, but it makes it seem like this role was assigned to the person, and the impact of this refactoring is not clear. Finally, the original description reads sloppy by closing the sentence with \u201Cetc\u201D."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Improvement areas visualized:"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:i,alt:"Image represents a text-based description of a software engineer's experience at Gibbery.  The main component is a bulleted list detailing four projects. The first bullet point describes working on the Billing team, developing microservices, but lacks specific details ('missing specifics'). The second bullet point details automating the error handling process for RESTful APIs, resulting in a reduced client support ticket rate, again lacking specific quantifiable results ('missing specifics, and could describe better what you did'). The third bullet point focuses on enhancing the MySap platform's customer experience through third-party API integrations (Zoom for meetings, Square for payments), again missing specific details ('missing specifics'). The final bullet point describes reverse engineering and analyzing undocumented legacy code to understand business logic, clean the code, isolate domains, and refactor it for a new billing service; however, feedback suggests the impact of this work needs clearer articulation and less passive phrasing ('impact of work could be more clear, passive statements, description makes it seem this person was told what to do').  The entire structure is a simple vertical list, with each bullet point describing a separate project and highlighting areas where more detail and active voice are needed.",width:"695",height:"243"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"After:"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:p,alt:"Image represents a bulleted list detailing the accomplishments of a Software Engineer at Gibbery, specifically within the Billing team.  The list comprises five bullet points, each describing a distinct project or improvement.  The first bullet point mentions the development, deployment, and operation of multiple microservices handling up to 200 queries per second (QPS). The second bullet point details improving the availability of a 'receipts Go microservice' from 99.8% to 99.9% by implementing a read-through cache layer using Redis. The third bullet point describes a reduction in support tickets by 80% for a major client through improved error handling in a Node.JS API, ensuring 100% of error codes are mapped to appropriate HTTP codes. The fourth bullet point highlights a 30% increase in customer conversion on a co-working platform (MySap) by integrating recurring payments and Zoom rooms using PHP and Go. Finally, the fifth bullet point focuses on enhancing the maintainability of the core billing engine by reverse engineering legacy code, isolating it into separate domains, and migrating it to independent services.  No visual connections or flows are present; the information is purely textual and hierarchical, organized as a list of accomplishments.",width:"695",height:"240"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"The \u201Cafter\u201D version adds specifics and describes what this person has done. Specifics include numbers, actions, and specific technologies. This re-edited version conveys that the person applying has moved the needle for the business, can get things done, and it mentions some specific technologies used on the project."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.hr,{}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.h2,{id:"dont-be-humble",children:"Don't Be Humble"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Your resume should sell the professional \u201Cyou\u201D for the position you are applying for. Don\u2019t claim untrue things, but do aim to paint a great picture of yourself for the audience: the recruiter and the hiring manager."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.ul,{children:[`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Talk about yourself, not your team. Avoid using \u201Cwe\u201D"})," and use the first person instead (in most cases, you can drop the \u201CI\u201D). The resume is about you, and what you have brought to the table in the past. And what the company would get by moving forward with you. Note for non-native English speakers that talking about the \u201Cwe\u201D is the norm when talking about achievements in some languages. Drop this\u2014in an English resume you should always go with the first-person approach."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsxs)(o.strong,{children:["Be concise but ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"not"})," humble."]})," Don\u2019t hide your achievements\u2014and when in doubt, inflating them on the borderline will hurt less than hiding them. Your CV needs to make you stand out from an already competitive crowd."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Make your side projects & open-source contributions shine"}),". If you built impressive projects or have great open source contributions, bring attention to these in those sections of your resume. Follow the results-impact-contribution model in calling this out, where you can, as opposed to just linking to your Github profile with no explanation or an app you\u2019ve built. If you don\u2019t sell it, the person reading the CV might not look at it in detail."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Do talk about extracurricular activities adjacent to tech"}),", at the end of your resume. Talk about what their impact was, how they were difficult, and link to high-quality resources that you have created. For example, if you\u2019ve organized a meetup with 100 participants, mention this. If you have a technical blog, link a specific, high-quality article the reader can read. Many resumes are dry, and showing off high-quality or standout activities can make a good resume even better. Be mindful of listing neutral extracurricular activities: ones that won\u2019t trigger bias. For example, when applying for a position in Manchester, UK, listing that you enjoy supporting Chelsea on their away games could trigger unconscious bias, as silly as it might sound."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Mention your learnings"}),". It is relevant to read about why and how you picked up a skill, how it impacted a current role or what you would like to achieve with this in the future. This is information that hands-on recruiters and hiring managers value hearing about, and it also makes the resume more valuable. For example, do mention if you picked up a technology to speed up a project, to stretch yourself, or other reasons. Learning new and useful skills proactively is a positive trait that hiring managers will appreciate. As a hiring manager, I wish I would see more of these learnings mentioned on resumes."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Don\u2019t highlight negatives"})," from past experiences. While you should not claim things that are not true, you don\u2019t need to list things that don\u2019t show you in a good light. Failed projects, low GPA scores and other things that come across as negative: you can safely leave these out."]}),`
`]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.hr,{}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Before and after: don\u2019t be humble"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Before:"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:a,alt:"Image represents a text-based resume section detailing work experience and projects.  The resume is structured into two main sections: 'Work experience' and 'Projects.'  The 'Work experience' section lists four bullet points describing past roles, including developing and maintaining an internal data serialization service adopted by other teams, building a web application for internal DevOps use, participating in sprint planning and strategy definition (sometimes leading meetings), and occasionally serving as a 'build master' for automated build processes. The 'Projects' section contains three bullet points. The first details volunteer work with Facebook Developer Circles Cairo, encompassing managing an online user group, organizing meetups/workshops/hackathons in Ghana, and building partnerships with local tech companies to foster developer ecosystem growth. The second describes a project, 'bareGo,' focused on learning software architecture and design using Go, emphasizing the applicability of concepts like SOLID and design patterns to other languages. The third project involves implementing API design principles and concepts using Node.js, labeled as 'RESTAPI with Node.'  No visual connections or information flow is present beyond the hierarchical bullet point structure organizing the listed experiences and projects.",width:"695",height:"377"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"These are the main issues with this version:"}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.ul,{children:[`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"Work experience: much of this undersells the candidate. There are multiple references to the team over the person. It talks passively (\u201Cteam member\u201D, \u201Ctook part\u201D) and adds squinting modifiers that make it seem like this person had little contribution (\u201Csometimes\u201D, \u201Coccasionally\u201D)."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"Volunteer experience. In the \u201Cbefore\u201D example, the candidate put quite a few details on their volunteer work: while this is not bad, it lacks data, and is verbose."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"bareGo: the description is quite casual (\u201Cthis project is usually\u2026\u201D) and it doesn\u2019t answer the question on why this project is interesting or challenging."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"RESTAPI project: this project has a very generic description. It could hide a \u201CHello World\u201D-like simple project, or a complex and interesting implementation."}),`
`]}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:P,alt:"Image represents a text-based resume outlining work experience and projects.  The resume is structured into two main sections: 'Work experience' and 'Projects.'  The 'Work experience' section lists four bullet points, each describing a role.  These descriptions often lack specific quantifiable achievements, indicated by parenthetical comments like '(missing specifics)' or '(achievements are underplayed).'  The 'Projects' section similarly uses bullet points, detailing volunteer work with Facebook Developer Circles Cairo and individual projects like 'bareGo' and a RESTAPI project.  These project descriptions are also criticized with parenthetical comments highlighting issues such as overly verbose descriptions, passive language, missing specifics, and a lack of explanation regarding the project's complexity or interesting aspects.  The comments act as annotations, pointing out areas for improvement in the resume's content and clarity.  No URLs or parameters are present; the information flow is purely textual, with each bullet point expanding on a specific experience or project.",width:"695",height:"487"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"After:"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:t,alt:"Image represents a tech resume structured into two main sections: 'Work experience' and 'Projects'.  The 'Work experience' section lists four bullet points detailing past roles.  The first describes building and promoting a Java service for data serialization, resulting in adoption by six other teams. The second details creating a Python-based monitoring tool (using Grafana for visualization) that prevented three major outages. The third bullet point highlights leading sprint planning sessions and taking on a 'build master' role, involving Jenkins and Groovy. The fourth bullet point focuses on self-learning Scala and Groovy to broaden programming language expertise, specifically mentioning the JVM and contrasting dynamic and static language characteristics. The 'Projects' section lists three bullet points. The first describes leading a Facebook Developer Circles community, organizing over 30 events. The second details creating a 'bareGo' Go framework for building efficient and scalable server-side applications, emphasizing its adherence to SOLID principles and providing a GitHub link. The third project showcases building a REST API using Node.js, highlighting the implementation of HATEOAS, caching, and content negotiation, also with a GitHub link.  Each bullet point within both sections provides concise descriptions of responsibilities, technologies used, and quantifiable results where applicable, often including URLs to GitHub repositories for project code.",width:"695",height:"413"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"The \u201Cafter\u201D version addresses several issues:"}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.ul,{children:[`

`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"Learnings are mentioned about why this person picked up a new technology like Groovy and Scala, what they achieved and hope to achieve with it."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"The volunteer section is far more focused on what the candidate did, and what the results were. \u201CI built up a local group of 500 developers, organized 30+ events and hackathons\u201D. `,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"The bareGo project now talks about the goal of the project, and the complex problems it solves, calling out the approach. As a hiring manager, I\u2019m ready to click on it to check it out."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"RESTAPI with Node also described the project well. It adds more details on the complexity behind the project"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.li,{children:"by listing the not-so-trivial elements like HATEOAS (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State) and Content Negotiation. Again, this description definitely grabs any technical hiring manager\u2019s interest."}),`
`]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.hr,{}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.h2,{id:"write-a-resume-for-that-job",children:"Write a Resume for That Job"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"If you have no problem getting recruiter calls with your resume, that\u2019s great news. It either means that your profile is a standout one, or that you have little competition in the roles that you apply for."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:["However, for most advertised positions, there are far more qualified job seekers for every tech position than there is headcount. This is great news for recruiters who are seeing lots of inbounds. However, it means you need to put in more work for your resume to grab their attention. To make your resume stand out, you need to write it for ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"that specific"})," job description."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Create a \u201Cmaster\u201D version of your resume that lists out lots of details in your work experience and projects sections. Use the results, impact and contribution language. Don't worry if this version goes beyond two pages\u2014as long as you\u2019ll be able to trim it down for each job description."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Then, create a version of your resume for the specific job description"}),", re-editing it so it uses similar language to what is in the job details. Remove examples that don\u2019t help you with this position, or move them out of the way. If a job description is for an Android role with a focus on Kotlin and you have Android, Kotlin, and web experience, make sure your resume shows your Android and Kotlin contributions\u2014and perhaps move the web experience further down."]}),`

She\u2019s interviewed more than 6,000 developers in 20 years and reviewed far more resumes, developers to startups and well-known tech companies. She shares her advice on how to tailor your resume:"}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsxs)(o.em,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"\u201CYou lose very little when removing non-relevant bullet points from your \u2018master\u2019 resume."})," Yet most are nervous to do so and opt to cram the content instead. Don\u2019t do this. Your resume is there to get you the interview. On the interview, you\u2019ll have the opportunity to talk about the various things that you did that are not on the resume. Be ruthless in removing things that don\u2019t help convey why you are a good fit for the position."]})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsxs)(o.em,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"One of the biggest \u2018secrets\u2019 in tailoring your resume to the job is to understand why the position was opened."})," Read the job description carefully.  Does it sound like they are hiring someone to build something brand new, as part of a new team, or is it more like there might be something to maintain, perhaps backfill someone? While it can be hard to tell exactly for large tech company job adverts, for small companies, here\u2019s a tip that will help."]})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"When reading a job advert of a small company, search for the LinkedIn profile of people working there and browse through them. Do they already have someone with the technical skills that the job advertises? If yes, then you\u2019d likely be working with this person. They will probably be on the interview loop as well.  And if there\u2019s no one with the languages and technologies the job is asking for, then they are probably hiring for something new. `,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:H,alt:"Image represents a job description for a Software Development Engineer at Amazon.  The top section poses a series of questions designed to gauge the applicant's interest in the role, highlighting aspects like working on critical payment systems, high availability, cutting-edge technology, and a collaborative environment.  The next section outlines the responsibilities of the position, including defining, designing, and implementing multi-tier distributed payment services, leading the team in developing new features for Amazon's payment platform, and estimating engineering effort and planning implementation rollouts, emphasizing the importance of functionality, performance, scalability, and reliability.  Following this, 'Basic Qualifications' lists required skills and experience, such as 4+ years of software development experience, computer science fundamentals in object-oriented design and data structures, algorithm design, and proficiency in at least one modern programming language (C, C++, Java, or Perl).  Finally, 'Preferred Qualifications' details desirable skills, including knowledge of software engineering best practices (coding standards, code reviews, etc.), project management experience from scoping to launch, and strong communication skills with various stakeholders.  The entire document is structured as a bulleted list, clearly separating responsibilities, basic requirements, and preferred qualifications.",width:"695",height:"683"})}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"This is not bad\u2014but it is clear how this description is a generic one. It does not reflect on the job description at all. After the changes, however, it reflects the language that this specific company or job listing uses."}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:v,alt:"Image represents a tech resume snippet focusing on a software engineer's experience at 'ThisCompany.'  The top section, titled 'Skills summary,' lists the candidate's proficiencies categorized into 'Languages' (Java, Go, Python, PHP), 'Databases' (Postgres, MongoDB, Redis), 'Tools' (AWS, Azure, GCP, Docker, Kafka, Git), and 'Other' (data structures, algorithms, distributed systems, engineering best practices). Below, the main body details three key accomplishments.  The first describes defining, designing, implementing, and rolling out a multi-tier customer profile service using Java, leading a four-engineer team, and its adoption by five other teams. The second highlights the introduction of best practices in reliability, monitoring, and alerting, including building a Grafana dashboard generator adopted by over ten teams, and improving testing practices via a reverse proxy. The third accomplishment details improving rollout processes by introducing a company-wide beta environment for testing new features with selected customers.  The keywords 'defined,' 'designed,' 'implemented,' 'rolled out,' 'best practices,' 'testing,' and 'rollouts' are emphasized, likely to highlight the candidate's contributions and impact.",width:"695",height:"256"})}),`

`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Top tech companies care far less about the specific languages used, but they do care about software engineering skills. Consultancies and agencies are more interested in very specific technologies and years of experience with those technologies. Tailor your resume for each."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Depending on what type of company and what type of developer role you are applying to, recruiters and hiring managers usually pay attention to different parts in your resume. Finally, the last paragraph highlights the management of project priorities, deadlines, and deliverables, along with the design, development, testing, deployment, maintenance, and enhancement of software solutions. `,(0,e.jsx)("table",{className:"inside-out",children:(0,e.jsx)("tbody",{children:(0,e.jsx)("tr",{children:(0,e.jsxs)("td",{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"From the inside out: grabbing the attention of an inbound sourcer at a tech company"})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Veronika Nora Nagy has recruited for Uber, as well as for startups and consultancies. She\u2019s reviewed thousands of incoming resumes at Uber when she acted as the inbound sourcer for tech positions. Here\u2019s her advice on how to grab the attention of the recruiter who first reads your resume:"}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"\u201CHighlight your experience relevant for the company that you are applying for. Things around scalability and reliability and similar experiences also would grab my attention."})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsxs)(o.em,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Whatever role you apply for, make sure that you clearly highlight the technologies you have worked with that are relevant for the role you are applying for."})," I also always find it a good sign when someone has worked with multiple programming languages and frameworks. It indicated that they are curious professionally, like to learn new things, and are open to new ideas."]})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Keep in mind that different cultures teach CV writing in different ways\u2014and a lot of these approaches might not work for international companies. Highlight your most relevant projects, the technologies you\u2019ve worked with, and be ready to talk about them when you get to that recruiter call."})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"I suggest only applying to companies hiring generalist engineers if you are open to learning and using new languages, or if you are comfortable with the existing stack of the company. In his guide ",(0,e.jsx)(o.a,{href:"http://pragmaticurl.com/konstanty-book-ttr",children:"The Developer\u2019s Guide to Getting a New Job"}),", he shares the following advice to follow when applying for roles that are geared towards a specific technology:"]}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"\u201CMention your top technologies first. Another no-go is naming every single technology you\u2019ve worked with. As an extreme example, there is absolutely no need to mention Microsoft Office. There are several ways you can do it. Your CV must have examples of each of your skills, as well as how, and when, and in what context you used them, and to what effect."})}),(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Concise job role summaries only. Three to four bullet points per role is perfect. Go for either a few bullets or a single paragraph of text covering each job summary. This normally includes your major tasks and responsibilities, and also key results. Put all the extra information on the last page. Keep sentences brief (seriously\u2014avoid long sentences) and don\u2019t list every single tool you have worked with (or every single project you\u2019ve done). Start sentences with action verbs to get points across quickly.\u201D"})})]})})})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.h2,{id:"keyword-stuffing",children:"Keyword Stuffing"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Keyword stuffing is a controversial but important topic. At companies where recruiters are not very technical, resume filtering is done by discarding resumes that do not have certain keywords. Recruiters and HR folks will often pattern match either based on expectations from the hiring manager or based on keywords that they have seen lead to offers in the past."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:[`Let's take a backend job description that explicitly says that the team uses Java for development. In most cases, resumes without a mention of "`,(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Java"}),'" or "',(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"backend"}),`" would be disregarded. For a team working on distributed systems, less hands-on tech recruiters might discard resumes that don't mention anything distributed\u2014even if they might describe working on such systems using phrases like \u201Cmicroservices\u201D, \u201Cmessaging queues\u201D or \u201Cglobally fault-tolerant systems\u201D. The recruiter is not a software engineer: they just look for the term "distributed". Note that this practice is less common at large companies with technical recruiters\u2014but it is a thing when screening is done by someone more junior.`]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"A workaround is to throw all possibly relevant keywords into the resume. This is called keyword stuffing. You can see it happening with this resume, for example:"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:x,alt:"Image represents a categorized list of technical skills, arranged in a hierarchical structure.  The top-level category is 'TECHNICAL SKILLS,' followed by sub-categories such as 'Programming,' 'Databases,' 'Back-end Technologies,' 'Front-end Technologies,' 'Testing,' 'Tools/IDEs,' 'API Paradigms,' 'Message Queues,' and 'Other Tools.' Each sub-category lists specific technologies, tools, or methodologies. For example, 'Programming' lists PHP, Java, JavaScript, Python, and SQL; 'Databases' lists MySQL, Oracle 11g, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and MongoDB; 'Back-end Technologies' includes Servlets, JSP, Spring MVC, Spring Boot, and more; and 'Front-end Technologies' lists HTML5, CSS3, jQuery, React.js, and others.  The items within each category are separated by commas, and categories are separated by double vertical bars ('||').  There's also a 'Learning' sub-category under 'Front-end Technologies' indicating ongoing learning in React Native, Redux, and Angular 7.  The arrangement visually groups related skills, allowing for a quick overview of the candidate's expertise across various aspects of software development.",width:"695",height:"201"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Keyword stuffing: an example of overdoing it"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"While the person doing this kind of keyword stuffing would think they\u2019ve \u201Ccovered\u201D all possible technologies, it can make the resume look unprofessional. For places where recruiters are less hands-on, this approach could work, as they might \u201Csee\u201D the keywords the job needs. For places where requirements are more clear and recruiters are technical, this strategy will work less well. Even if a recruiter would decide to proceed, as a hiring manager, I would consider putting this resume in the \u201Cmaybe\u201D pile."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"So how do you include keywords relevant for the position, while also keeping your resume professional? You do this by including the most relevant keywords\u2014technologies and frameworks\u2014in your resume, but do this in a human-readable way."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:`A good way to have keywords present in your resume, while also keeping it professional is to have a short "Technologies", \u201CSkills\u201D or \u201CLanguages and Technologies\u201D section where you list the technologies you are familiar with and are relevant for the job. In your Experience section, sprinkle specifics on the relevant technologies you've used in projects. It's fine to mention the same technology both in the technologies section, as well as under the specific part of the experience. But do ensure the resume stays easy to read.`}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Let\u2019s go back to how to improve that part in the previous resume. We\u2019ll cut down the technologies listed to be relevant to the job description, and mention technologies that were relevant in getting certain projects done:"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(D,{children:(0,e.jsx)(c,{src:_,alt:"Image represents a tech resume snippet showcasing skills and work experience.  The top section, titled 'LANGUAGES AND TECHNOLOGIES,' lists programming languages (PHP, JavaScript, Python) and frameworks (Laravel, React, Angular) under 'Languages and frameworks,' database technologies (MySQL, MongoDB), cloud services (AWS), and version control (Git) under 'Technologies and tools,' and finally,  'Data structures and algorithms, API design, unit testing' under 'Other.'  Below, the 'WORK EXPERIENCE' section details a role as 'Backend Software Engineer' at 'SmallComp.'  This section includes bullet points describing accomplishments: re-architecting a RESTful API for a mobile client using PHP and Laravel; improving customer conversion by 30% through an improved checkout flow; migrating a MongoDB database to MySQL; and building the frontend using React.  The technologies used in each accomplishment are highlighted, demonstrating a connection between the skills listed at the top and the practical application in the work experience.",width:"695",height:"200"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"Sensible keywords: cutting down to ones relevant to the position, bringing examples in the work experience section"})}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"The result is a cleaner resume that still has key technologies listed\u2014in fact, it reinforces the ones that the candidate has more hands-on experience with those technologies."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.p,{children:["Recruiters will scan for different keywords for different job positions. This is another reason you'll want to create a custom resume for ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"that"})," job description. You can tailor your wording and technologies you are proficient with, so recruiters keep reading after they've seen key technologies mentioned."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Don't forget: your goal is to get through the initial resume screening where most inbound applications without referrals fail\u2014many of whom are actually qualified for the job, but their resume does not tell the story."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.h2,{id:"recap-actions-to-improve-your-resume",children:"Recap: Actions to Improve Your Resume"}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"In this chapter, we\u2019ve gone through ways to have your resume stand out from the crowd. Being specific about your results and impact, and using numbers to convey the value you created is a massive differentiator."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"Tailoring your resume for the specific position is even more so. Different companies do care about different areas: tech companies hiring general software engineers are more interested in seeing a breadth of skills, while companies hiring for a specific technology care more about expertise with the stack they work with. Tailoring your resume with keywords, doing sensible \u201Ckeyword stuffing\u201D is an additional step worth considering, to make sure the resume makes it through the ATS system if you don\u2019t have a referral."}),`
`,(0,e.jsx)(o.p,{children:"To make your resume stands out, carry out the following checks."}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.ol,{children:[`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Numbers and impact in your resume."})," Do you have some, or most of your results expressed with numbers or percentages? If not, aim to change this. The more specifics, and the more measurable the impact, the more you convey your results."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Are you using active language?"})," Use active verbs that convey what ",(0,e.jsx)(o.em,{children:"you"})," did, as opposed to things that happened, in a passive setting."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Are you mentioning technologies in your examples?"})," On top of numbers, are you being specific on the tools that you used to achieve some of the results? You don\u2019t need to do this for every part, but repeating technologies that you also list on your languages and technologies section reinforces that you are hands-on with these."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Have you customized your resume for that specific job, creating a specific version?"})," You\u2019ll want to have your resume reflect on the job you are applying for. Are you using similar language to the job description to describe your activities? Are you explicitly calling out technologies and languages in the job description that you are proficient with? Are you using similar active verbs to those the job description contains? Tailoring your resume, together with expressing the impact of your work, are the two most impactful changes you can make."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Are you talking about yourself, not talking about \u201Cwe\u201D or \u201Cthe team\u201D?"})," Make sure your resume is about your achievements. Don\u2019t be humble\u2014err on the side of taking credit for the work that you were involved in."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Do you mention impactful, complex or interesting side projects?"})," Are you describing these projects in such a way that it\u2019s easy to understand why they are relevant? Don\u2019t leave off some of your additional achievements that add value to your resume."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Are you applying for a generalist engineering role, or one focused on one specific technology?"})," Differentiate what type of position you are writing your resume for. The two types of companies should have different resumes. Tailor your resume accordingly."]}),`
`,(0,e.jsxs)(o.li,{children:[(0,e.jsx)(o.strong,{children:"Have you \u201Cstuffed\u201D keywords in a sensible way?"})," When comparing the job description and your resume, does your resume contain what could be the main \u201Ckeywords\u201D that recruiters will look for? Does your resume contain the key languages and technologies listed, the name of the position and the tech stack? If the position asks for a minimum number of years\u2019 experience, does your resume convey this information, directly or indirectly?"]}),`
